name: Ravana
description: Ravana, the Rakshasa king of Lanka, is a prominent figure in Hindu mythology, primarily known from the epic Ramayana. He is often depicted with ten heads and twenty arms, symbolizing his extensive knowledge, power, and mastery over various scriptures and arts. Born to the sage Vishrava and the Rakshasi Kaikesi, Ravana inherited both Brahminical wisdom and Rakshasa strength. Despite his demoniac lineage, he was a learned scholar, a skilled warrior, and a master of the Vedas. His devotion to Shiva granted him immense boons, making him virtually invincible. However, his arrogance and relentless pursuit of power led him to commit grave injustices, ultimately leading to his downfall at the hands of Rama. Ravana embodies the complexities of human nature, showcasing how even immense knowledge and power can be corrupted by ego and ambition. He is a crucial antagonist in the Ramayana, whose actions drive the narrative and provide a platform for the triumph of dharma (righteousness) over adharma (unrighteousness).
subtitle: King of Lanka

keyMyths.title: The Acquisition of Immortality
keyMyths.description: Ravana performed severe penance to appease Lord Brahma. As a result of his unwavering devotion, Brahma offered him a boon. Ravana, driven by his ambition for immortality, asked for protection from gods, demons, and other celestial beings. However, he neglected to include humans in his request, believing them to be insignificant. This oversight ultimately became his undoing, as Lord Rama, an avatar of Vishnu in human form, was able to defeat him, highlighting the importance of humility and foresight.

keyMyths.title: The Abduction of Sita
keyMyths.description: Driven by revenge for the humiliation of his sister, Shurpanakha, Ravana plotted to abduct Sita, Rama's wife. He enlisted the help of Maricha, a Rakshasa skilled in illusion, to lure Rama and Lakshmana away from Sita. Maricha, disguised as a golden deer, captivated Sita's attention, prompting Rama to pursue it. With Rama and Lakshmana gone, Ravana, disguised as a mendicant, approached Sita and abducted her, carrying her away to Lanka in his flying chariot, the Pushpaka Vimana. This act of aggression set in motion the epic battle between Rama and Ravana.

keyMyths.title: Ravana's Defeat and Death
keyMyths.description: The climax of the Ramayana revolves around the epic battle between Rama's army and Ravana's forces in Lanka. Despite possessing immense power and numerous boons, Ravana was ultimately defeated by Rama. The battle was prolonged and fierce, with both sides suffering heavy losses. However, Rama's unwavering righteousness and divine power proved too strong for Ravana to overcome. Ravana was finally slain by Rama's arrow, marking the victory of good over evil and the restoration of dharma.

sources.author: Valmiki

associations.name: Ten
associations.significance: Represents his ten heads, symbolizing knowledge of the six Shastras and four Vedas.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Associated with his golden complexion and the opulence of Lanka.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Represents his intense power, anger, and destructive capabilities.

associations.name: Power
associations.significance: Symbolizes his immense strength, both physical and magical, and his desire for dominion.

associations.name: Rakshasa
associations.significance: Represents his demoniac nature and lineage, embodying strength and fear.

associations.name: Lunar Eclipse
associations.significance: Connected to his ability to manipulate celestial events and his association with darkness.

worshipPractices.name: Ravana Shrines
worshipPractices.description: In some parts of India and Sri Lanka, Ravana is worshipped as a powerful and learned figure. Temples dedicated to him exist, where he is revered for his knowledge of Ayurveda, astrology, and his devotion to Shiva. These practices often involve rituals and offerings to appease him and seek his blessings for wisdom and strength.

festivals.name: Vijayadashami
festivals.description: While Vijayadashami celebrates Rama's victory over Ravana, some communities, especially in Sri Lanka, observe it with mixed emotions, acknowledging Ravana's scholarship and valor alongside his defeat. Effigies are burned to symbolize the triumph of good over evil, but also serve as a reminder of Ravana's complex character.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ravana remains a complex and controversial figure in modern culture. He is often depicted in literature, art, and theater, representing both villainy and intellectual prowess. Modern interpretations explore his motivations and challenge traditional narratives, portraying him as a symbol of resistance against oppressive forces or as a tragic hero with admirable qualities overshadowed by his hubris.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Ravana's birth is steeped in mythological significance. He was born to the sage Vishrava, a learned Brahmin, and Kaikesi, a Rakshasi princess. This unique parentage endowed him with both Brahminical wisdom and Rakshasa strength. His grandfather was the illustrious sage Pulastya, one of the Prajapatis. Ravana's birth name was Dasagriva, referring to his ten heads, which he acquired through intense penance and self-sacrifice to Lord Brahma. According to legend, he offered his heads one by one into a sacrificial fire, and each time Brahma restored them, impressed by his devotion. This act earned him boons of invincibility against gods, demons, and other celestial beings, save for humans. This perceived invulnerability fueled his arrogance and ambition, setting the stage for his eventual downfall. His lineage and the circumstances of his birth highlight the complex interplay of divine and demonic forces in Hindu mythology.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The Ramayana is the central narrative in which Ravana's story unfolds. He is primarily known for abducting Sita, Rama's wife, and holding her captive in Lanka, thus instigating the epic battle between Rama and himself. Other significant myths include his acquisition of Lanka from Kubera, his half-brother, through treachery and force. He is also known for his devotion to Lord Shiva, to whom he offered his heads as a sacrifice. His arrogance and insatiable desire for power led him to challenge the gods and conquer various realms, establishing his dominance over the three worlds. Despite his negative portrayal, Ravana is also revered for his knowledge of statecraft, medicine (Ayurveda), and astrology. His expertise in the Vedas and his musical talents are often acknowledged, adding layers to his complex character. These stories collectively portray him as a formidable antagonist with both admirable qualities and fatal flaws.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Ravana's legacy extends beyond the Ramayana, influencing art, literature, and cultural practices across South and Southeast Asia. He is a recurring figure in various forms of media, often depicted in plays, movies, and television series. His character has been interpreted in diverse ways, ranging from a purely evil villain to a misunderstood hero who challenged the established order. In some communities, particularly in Sri Lanka, Ravana is viewed as a national hero and a symbol of resistance against foreign invaders. His knowledge of Ayurveda and astrology is still respected, and his contributions to these fields are acknowledged. Modern interpretations often explore the psychological complexities of his character, examining his motivations and highlighting the consequences of unchecked ambition and pride. He remains a potent symbol of both the dangers of ego and the complexities of human nature.

shortDescription: Rakshasa king of Lanka from the Ramayana. Depicted with ten heads, representing his knowledge and power. Known for abducting Sita and his devotion to Shiva.
